text_segment: Wilderness survival has many hazards including getting lost, extreme weather, injuries, and dangerous animals. You should anticipate these hazards by planning ahead, telling others your plans, and bringing proper gear.

text_segment: Hypothermia happens when your body loses heat faster than it can produce it. Signs include shivering, confusion, and slurred speech. Treat it by getting the person warm, dry, and into shelter.
question: Explain what hypothermia is and how to treat it.
Active: Hypothermia occurs when the body loses heat too quickly.
Progressive: Hypothermia is occurring when the body loses heat too quickly.
Passive: Heat is lost too quickly by the body during hypothermia.
Subordinate: When someone has hypothermia, get them warm and dry.

text_segment: Heat exhaustion and heat stroke happen when your body overheats. Signs include heavy sweating, weakness, and confusion. Move the person to shade, give them water, and cool them down with wet cloths.

Active: Rescuers treat heat exhaustion by moving victims to shade and cooling them.

Passive: Heat exhaustion is treated by moving to shade and cooling down.

text_segment: Frostbite happens when skin and tissue freeze in very cold temperatures. Fingers, toes, ears, and nose are most at risk. Warm the affected area gradually with body heat, not hot water or rubbing.

Active: Frostbite freezes skin and tissue in cold temperatures.

text_segment: Dehydration happens when your body loses more water than it takes in. Signs include thirst, dark urine, dizziness, and fatigue. Prevent it by drinking water regularly, even before you feel thirsty.

text_segment: The seven priorities for wilderness survival are: positive attitude, first aid, shelter, fire, signaling, water, and food. These are listed in order of importance. A positive attitude is most important for survival.

text_segment: To avoid panic when lost, stop moving and sit down. Use the word STOP: Sit, Think, Observe, Plan. Take deep breaths to calm yourself. Remember that rescuers are looking for you if people know where you went.

text_segment: In cold and snowy conditions, shelter and warmth are your top priorities. Build a shelter to block wind and insulate from the ground. Layer your clothing and keep dry. Build a fire if possible to stay warm.

text_segment: In windy conditions, wind can steal body heat quickly. Find shelter behind rocks, trees, or hills to block the wind. Build a windbreak if no natural shelter exists. Keep your clothing layers tight to trap warm air.

text_segment: At or on water, staying afloat and getting out of cold water are priorities. If your boat capsizes, stay with it because it is easier to spot. Use a life jacket and signal for help. Hypothermia is a major danger in cold water.

text_segment: Ground-to-air signals are large symbols made on the ground for aircraft to see. An X means you need medical help. A V means you need assistance. An arrow shows your direction of travel. An I means you need supplies.

text_segment: Protect yourself from animals by storing food away from your camp. Hang food from trees to keep bears away. Watch where you step to avoid snakes. Use insect repellent and check for ticks after being outdoors.

text_segment: In cold weather, dress in layers: a base layer to wick moisture, a middle layer for insulation, and an outer layer to block wind and rain. Avoid cotton because it loses warmth when wet. Keep your head covered.

question: Describe proper clothing for wet conditions.

text_segment: In a survival situation, it is usually unwise to eat wild plants. Many plants are poisonous and hard to identify correctly. Eating the wrong plant can make you very sick. Water and shelter are more important than food.
question: Explain why eating wild plants can be dangerous.
Active: Many wild plants are poisonous and hard to identify.
Progressive: Many wild plants are being poisonous and dangerous.
Passive: Poisonous plants are found throughout the wilderness.
Subordinate: Because many plants are poisonous, avoid eating wild plants.

order_passive_beginner_1: poisonous plants,are found,throughout the,wilderness,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,many plants are poisonous,,,avoid eating,wild plants,.
order_subordinate_beginner_2: avoid eating,wild plants,because,many plants are poisonous,.

text_segment: Food is the last priority in wilderness survival. A healthy person can survive for weeks without food but only days without water. Focus on shelter, fire, signaling, and water before worrying about food.
question: Explain why food is the lowest survival priority.
Active: People can survive weeks without food but only days without water.
Progressive: People are surviving weeks without food.
Passive: Weeks can be survived without food, but not without water.
Subordinate: Because water is more urgent, focus on it before food.

order_subordinate_beginner_1: because,water is more urgent,,,focus on it,before food,.

text_segment: Three of anything is a universal distress signal. Three whistle blasts, three fires, or three gunshots tell others you need help. Space your signals apart so rescuers can see or hear each one clearly.
question: Explain the universal distress signal.
Active: Three of anything signals distress and calls for help.
Progressive: Three signals are calling for help.
Passive: Distress is signaled by using three of anything.
Subordinate: To signal for help, use three whistle blasts or three fires.

order_passive_beginner_1: distress,is signaled,by using,three of anything,.
order_subordinate_beginner_1: to,signal for help,,,use three whistle blasts,or three fires,.
order_subordinate_beginner_2: use three whistle blasts,or three fires,to,signal for help,.

text_segment: A whistle is one of the best signaling tools. Sound travels farther than your voice, and blowing a whistle uses less energy than shouting. Carry a whistle on a cord around your neck so you always have it.
question: Explain why a whistle is important for signaling.
Active: Whistles carry sound farther than shouting and save energy.
Progressive: Whistles are carrying sound farther than shouting.
Passive: Sound is carried farther by whistles than by shouting.
Subordinate: Because whistles save energy, carry one around your neck.

order_subordinate_beginner_2: carry one,around your neck,because,whistles save energy,.

text_segment: Smoke signals can attract aircraft and distant rescuers. Add green leaves or grass to a fire to create thick white smoke. Dark smoke comes from burning rubber or plastic. Build signal fires in open areas.
question: Explain how to create smoke signals.
Active: Green leaves create thick white smoke for signaling.
Progressive: Green leaves are creating thick smoke for signals.
Passive: Thick white smoke is created by adding green leaves to fires.
Subordinate: To create smoke signals, add green leaves to your fire.

order_subordinate_beginner_2: add green leaves,to your fire,to,create smoke signals,.

text_segment: When lost, it is usually best to stay where you are. Moving makes it harder for rescuers to find you. Stay near your last known location if people know where you were going. Make yourself visible.
question: Explain why staying put is often best when lost.
Active: Staying put helps rescuers find you more easily.
Progressive: Staying put is helping rescuers find lost people.
Passive: Lost people are found more easily when they stay put.
Subordinate: When lost, stay put so rescuers can find you.

order_subordinate_beginner_2: stay put,so rescuers can find you,when,lost,.

text_segment: The will to survive is the most important factor in wilderness survival. People with strong determination to live are more likely to survive. Stay positive, think clearly, and never give up hope.
question: Explain why the will to survive is so important.
Active: The will to survive helps people overcome difficult situations.
Progressive: The will to survive is helping people overcome challenges.
Passive: Difficult situations are overcome by those with the will to survive.
Subordinate: Because determination matters most, never give up hope.
